title: Other Management Consulting Services

narrative.executive_view: 541618 is a residual code: it excludes general, HR, marketing and logistics consulting, while expressly including telecommunications and utilities management consulting [S1]. That boundary makes a generic code-level underwriting thesis weak. I use telecom expense management (TEM), utility-management and niche specialty advisory as working subsegments, but do not claim a measured dominant revenue cluster. The attractive contingency-fee TEM slice can retain a share of savings; the same analytical product is also highly susceptible to client self-service AI. With an economically anomalous supplied labor share of 1.3991, this is a low-confidence, pressured terminal-demand screen rather than a platform thesis.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: Management analysts gather information, analyze data, develop solutions and prepare reports, while also interviewing people, consulting and maintaining relationships [S2]. In a TEM or utility-audit workflow, invoice ingestion, anomaly detection, benchmarking and draft reports are the most automatable pieces; carrier negotiation, disputed-charge resolution and client accountability remain less so. The supplied 4-digit 5416 role mix is especially weak here because this residual code can contain niches with very different labor and technology intensity.
narrative.value_capture: TEM providers use several fee models. A live provider's pricing description lists contingency arrangements that take 20-50% of recovered savings, as well as fixed-hourly, fixed-license and fixed-scope models [S6]. Contingency work can preserve economics when the provider continues to originate verified savings, but it also makes AI substitution more existential: if the client runs the analysis internally, there may be no fee pool. I therefore give distribution credit only for the contingency/managed component and assign a modest moat because the source is a vendor description, not a code-wide mix survey.
narrative.adoption_timing: Professional services are reported at about 33% firm AI adoption in the BTOS synthesis [S3], while Census reports less than 20% AI use among firms with four or fewer employees in its 2025-26 collection [S4]. The supplied dataset says the great majority of 541618 firms are under five employees, so I use 20% current adoption and four years to 50% effective adoption. This deliberately treats broad-sector adoption as an upper anchor and does not use the small target count, owner age or consolidation to accelerate A.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The supplied estimate has only 256 firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band, so buyability is not the same as an abundant platform funnel. Professional-services lower-middle-market ranges are 2.5x-4.5x for $1-3M EBITDA and 4.0x-6.0x for $3-10M, with utilization and key-person dependence named drivers [S5]. I use 3.25x entry and 3.75x exit only as a cautious de-risking case within those broad ranges. The micro tier also has a different sale metric: BizBuySell reports an average 2.52 cash-flow multiple for service businesses in 2025 [S7], which is not interchangeable with EBITDA and reinforces the need to normalize owner compensation.
narrative.terminal_demand: The code's telecom and utilities inclusion gives a plausible human-survival channel: ongoing representation, exception handling and carrier or utility negotiation. But the core savings-discovery product is informational, and TEM contingency pricing explicitly pays for billing-error and cost savings [S6]. Mature AI in a client's hands could remove rather than merely cheapen that product. I classify terminal demand as PRESSURED, not MELTING, because specialty representation and managed recurring work may persist, but the code-level evidence cannot establish their prevalence.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: This is the most uncertain packet in the pair. Official NAICS establishes the residual boundary but not revenue shares [S1]; the selected TEM cluster is a hypothesis, not a demonstrated dominant subsegment. The 1.3991 supplied labor share is economically implausible and likely reflects a cross-vintage/classification mismatch, so V should be treated as a sensitivity rather than a literal labor-cost forecast. National owner-transition evidence and broad professional-services multiple data are disclosed proxies. Diligence must identify the actual subsegment, fee basis, contract recurrence, data assets, client self-service capability and normalized EBITDA before buying.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual
sources.what_it_supports: Official 541618 residual-code definition, exclusions and explicit inclusion of telecommunications and utilities management consulting.

sources.what_it_supports: Management-analyst tasks spanning analytical/reporting work as well as interviews, consultation and interpersonal activities.

sources.what_it_supports: BTOS-based professional-services AI adoption of about 33% and the difference between adoption measures.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.what_it_supports: Census BTOS evidence that less than 20% of firms with four or fewer employees reported AI use and the 2025 question scope.

sources.title: Windsor Drake EBITDA Multiples by Industry: 2026 Data
sources.what_it_supports: Q1 2026 professional-services lower-middle-market EV/adjusted-EBITDA ranges and valuation drivers by EBITDA size.

sources.what_it_supports: TEM pricing models including 20-50% contingency fees on savings, fixed-hourly, fixed-license and fixed-scope fees.

sources.title: Business Acquisitions Stabilize as Buyers Get Selective
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 service-business sale activity and average 2.52 cash-flow multiple; this is a micro-service-market comparator, not an EBITDA or 541618 multiple.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.what_it_supports: National owner-age and transition-planning evidence used only as a disclosed proxy for small-business succession conditions.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541618 ($14,897,754,779) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541618 ($11,352,826,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $247,727 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541618); class <5: 11,518 firms, avg revenue/firm $353,982 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $55,398 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 661 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,508,079 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $236,014 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 283 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,159,795 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $494,508 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 216 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,167,644 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,434,736 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,072,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,393,362 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $81,481,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,751,894 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 256
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Analytical, invoice and reporting workflows are AI-exposed, but code composition and the supplied 4-digit occupational proxy are highly uncertain.
caveats: 541618 is a residual code; no actual dominant revenue subsegment is established.
The supplied labor share is anomalous and should not be read as literal economics.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Audit analysis, benchmarking and reporting automate, while negotiation and client discovery remain.
Owner coordination and relationships remain central despite administrative automation.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research and proposal content automate; selling judgment remains.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting materials automate; relationship-led sales remain.
Tracking and reporting automate; client coordination remains.
Routine intake, scheduling and status service are strongly automatable.
Data and operations work is AI-assisted with exception judgment retained.
AI assists implementation and scripts, but systems accountability remains.
Invoice and reconciliation workflows are comparatively repeatable, with review retained.
Administrative processes automate while sensitive advisory work remains.
Unknown residual blends specialty advisory with administration; a midrange judgment avoids assuming all TEM work.

pi_dist.rationale: Contingency TEM can retain a share of savings, but fixed-hourly and client self-service routes pass savings through; midpoint reflects an unknown mix.
caveats: Vendor pricing page demonstrates possible mechanisms, not 541618 revenue mix.

pi_moat.rationale: Specialty negotiation and managed service can defend economics, but generic audit analytics are readily substitutable.
caveats: No code-level evidence measures proprietary data, switching costs or client retention.

t50_years.rationale: Professional-services adoption is high while micro-firm use is lower; effective workflow adoption in the residual code is likely slower than broad-sector tool use.
caveats: Both anchors are broad proxies and use different populations/question wording.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Census reports less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees; 0.20 is a rounded upper-bound proxy given this code's micro-firm concentration.
caveats: Not a 541618 measurement; four-or-fewer firms are not identical to the target band.

historical_analogs.value: Contingency savings work links fees to measured outcomes, while fixed-hourly audit work is more exposed to labor-hour compression; no quantified historical analogue is asserted.
historical_analogs.rationale: TEM price mechanisms support the directional distinction but not a historical causal estimate.
caveats: Evidence is a provider description and may not represent the code.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: National evidence reports 52.3% of employer businesses owned by people 55 or older; 25% is a conservative unverified 60-plus proxy for a code dominated by micro firms.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Gallup documents national transition uncertainty and large shares of businesses that expect closure or have no long-term plan.
caveats: National evidence includes nonemployers and is not a specialty-consulting survey.

active_consolidators.rationale: The broad professional-services transaction market supports some buyer interest, but no qualifying 541618 consolidator count was found.
caveats: A code-level count is not established.

buy_mult.rationale: Low end of professional-services EBITDA ranges, corroborated directionally by the smaller service-business cash-flow market, reflects residual-code and key-person risk.
caveats: S7 uses cash flow/SDE, not EBITDA; neither source is a 541618 closed-deal set, and owner compensation must be normalized.

exit_mult.rationale: Half-turn improvement assumes recurring managed contracts and reduced owner dependency, not general market expansion.
caveats: No exit premium exists without verified contract recurrence and transferable client relationships.

pricing_structure.value: Unknown code mix; TEM can be contingency, fixed-hourly, license or fixed-scope, while specialty advisory may be project or managed-service based.
pricing_structure.rationale: Official code inclusion plus TEM pricing evidence establish possible mechanisms, not the dominant 541618 model.
caveats: Dominant revenue segment is a hypothesis and must be identified before underwriting capture.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role-weighted uplift is presented with low confidence and a four-year t50 because broad tool use is not equivalent to effective redesign in a micro-heavy residual code.

terminal_value.justification: Human representation and exception handling may survive, but savings discovery and invoice analysis can be internalized by AI-equipped clients, especially under contingency pricing.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Not confirmed: selected TEM/utility subsegments, current adoption, owner age, consolidation and multiples are disclosed proxies rather than exact 541618 measurements.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Fixed-hourly and project work should reprice as AI reduces labor; contingency work can retain savings only while the provider remains necessary to find and realize them.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 1.3991 labor share exceeds one and combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts, signalling a likely residual-code classification/vintage mismatch; roles are 4-digit 5416.
sensitivity: Do not interpret V as a literal percentage of revenue savings.
A normalized labor share nearer sibling consulting codes would materially lower the value thesis.

market_boundary.selection_basis: Official definition explicitly includes telecom and utilities; no revenue-share evidence establishes dominance.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 4.0 to about 1.43 across the stated range.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Dataset value 1.3991 is economically anomalous; sibling-like normalized level unknown
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: V and uplift are not decision-grade until the data mismatch is resolved.
C depends on whether actual revenue is contingency, managed service or effort-based.
M changes materially with normalization and the actual specialty/contract mix.

reviewer_note: Every cited URL was reopened on 2026-07-21. The packet does not claim that TEM is the measured dominant 541618 segment; it is an explicitly flagged working hypothesis.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
